1.operational_reality: Simple to operate, simple to debug. The ceiling is real but often higher than people think — a well-tuned single server can handle more than most startups will ever need.
1.what_seniors_argue_about: When to leave single-server. Too early = wasted complexity. Too late = emergency migration under pressure.

key_numbers.modern_server_connections: 10K-100K concurrent with event loop, 1K-10K with threads

2.operational_reality: Easy to scale out/in. Easy to deploy (rolling update). The external state store becomes your new single point of failure.
2.what_seniors_argue_about: Where to put state: Redis (fast, less durable), PostgreSQL (durable, slower), DynamoDB (scalable, expensive). Each has different failure characteristics.

key_numbers.rolling_deploy_time: minutes for small fleets, 30-60 min for thousands of instances

3.operational_reality: WAL provides crash safety but adds write latency (fsync cost). LSM provides high write throughput but adds read amplification and compaction burden. B-tree provides balanced read/write but has write amplification.
3.what_seniors_argue_about: fsync per transaction vs group commit. Throughput vs durability. Whether you can afford to lose the last 10ms of writes.

used_by: Redis in front of PostgreSQL (most web apps)
4.operational_reality: Works great until invalidation is wrong. Every caching bug manifests as 'users see wrong data' — the hardest kind of bug to debug because it's intermittent and state-dependent.
4.what_seniors_argue_about: TTL duration: too short = no benefit, too long = stale data. Event-driven invalidation: more correct but adds coupling and failure modes to the invalidation path.

5.operational_reality: Decouples producer from consumer in time. But introduces eventual consistency, ordering challenges, and the 'exactly once' illusion. Dead-letter queues are not optional.
5.what_seniors_argue_about: Kafka vs RabbitMQ (log vs queue). At-least-once + idempotency vs at-most-once + loss acceptance. Whether 'exactly once' marketing claims are real (they aren't without end-to-end idempotency).

key_numbers.sqs_latency: 1-10ms typical, up to 20s for delayed messages
key_numbers.consumer_lag_alert: seconds for real-time, minutes for batch — depends on SLO

6.operational_reality: Leader failover is the hardest operational problem. Automatic failover gets it wrong 5-30% of the time in split-brain scenarios. Manual failover is safer but slower.
6.what_seniors_argue_about: Automatic vs manual failover. Both have killed production systems. Synchronous replication (safe but slow) vs async (fast but data loss possible on failover).

key_numbers.data_loss_window_async: last N seconds of replication lag

7.operational_reality: Choosing the wrong partition key is a multi-month mistake. You can't easily re-partition without downtime or significant engineering investment. Hot partitions are the #1 operational issue.
7.what_seniors_argue_about: Whether to use database-managed sharding (CockroachDB, Vitess) or application-level sharding. The answer depends on team size and operational maturity.

key_numbers.shard_count_starting: 4-16 shards (room to grow without immediate rebalancing)
key_numbers.rebalancing_time: hours to days for large datasets
key_numbers.cross_shard_query_cost: N× single-shard latency (fan-out to all shards)

8.operational_reality: Consensus adds latency to every write (majority must acknowledge). Operational simplicity comes from using an existing system (etcd, ZooKeeper) rather than implementing your own. Never implement your own consensus in production.
8.what_seniors_argue_about: Whether you actually NEED consensus or whether eventual consistency with conflict resolution is sufficient. Most systems need consensus in fewer places than architects think.

key_numbers.cluster_size: 3 or 5 nodes (never even — defeats majority logic)

9.operational_reality: Sagas are operationally complex. You need: saga state store, compensation logic for every step, monitoring for stuck sagas, manual intervention tools for compensation failures. Most teams underestimate this operational burden by 5-10x.
9.what_seniors_argue_about: Orchestration (central coordinator) vs choreography (event-driven). Orchestration is debuggable but creates a single point of failure. Choreography is resilient but debugging across services is painful.

key_numbers.intermediate_state_duration: milliseconds to minutes depending on async boundaries

10.operational_reality: Observability is expensive — both in engineering time and infrastructure cost. Sampling is necessary at scale (can't store every trace). The 80/20 rule: 20% of the instrumentation gives 80% of the debugging value.
10.what_seniors_argue_about: Structured events (Honeycomb philosophy: one wide event per request) vs three separate pillars. Cost of full-fidelity vs sampling. Whether logs are even necessary if you have good metrics and traces.

11.pattern: Circuit Breaker + Bulkhead + Retry with Jitter
used_by: Netflix Hystrix (deprecated but pattern lives on)
11.operational_reality: Resilience patterns are configuration-heavy. Wrong timeouts cause more outages than they prevent. The interaction between circuit breakers and retries can create unexpected behavior (circuit breaker opens because retries are flooding it).
11.what_seniors_argue_about: Whether to build resilience into the application or the infrastructure (service mesh). Application-level gives more control. Infrastructure-level is consistent but opaque.

key_numbers.circuit_breaker_open_duration: 10-60 seconds (should be close to dependency recovery time)
key_numbers.jitter_range: 0 to max_delay (full jitter) or base to max (equal jitter)

12.operational_reality: Capacity estimates are always wrong — the question is whether they're wrong by 2x or 10x. The value isn't the number itself but the thinking process that identifies which resource hits the ceiling first.
12.what_seniors_argue_about: How much headroom to maintain. 2x headroom is expensive. 1.2x headroom is risky. The right answer depends on how fast you can scale and how tolerant your users are of degradation.
